topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull, heavy thud. I stared at the screen, the bright white text blurring slightly. The words—'regret to inform you'—felt like a physical slap. Every line of my essay, every late night spent poring over research, seemed to evaporate into thin air. I slowly closed the laptop, the click of the lid sounding deafening in the quiet dorm room. My hands began to tremble, and I pressed them hard against my thighs, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, alarming lightness in my chest. I replayed the interview in my mind, searching for the moment I faltered, the sentence that sounded too hesitant, the answer that fell flat. It was all there, a tapestry of missed opportunities woven from my own inadequacy. I stood up, pacing the small space, my breath catching in shallow gasps.

[story 2]

"No." The single word echoed in the silence of the library carrel. I picked up the rejection letter, the heavy cardstock feeling impossibly cold beneath my fingertips. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the fluorescent lights above seem to pulse with malicious intent. I crumpled the paper, the sound sharp and unforgivable. I wanted to throw it across the room, to destroy the evidence of my failure. Instead, I smoothed it out again, meticulously, as if careful handling could somehow reverse the decision. My jaw tightened until my molars ached. I looked at my reflection in the darkened monitor—hollow eyes, skin stretched tight over bone. Why hadn't I been enough? The thought coiled in my stomach, a tight, painful knot that refused to loosen.

[story 3]

I kept running the numbers in my head, trying to find the mathematical flaw in the process, the variable I miscalculated. It wasn't about the grades; they were impeccable. It was about the narrative, the supposed spark of passion that the committee had evidently failed to ignite. I walked to the window, watching the campus quad where other students laughed, their easy camaraderie seeming impossibly distant and effortless. A bitter taste coated my tongue. I felt a profound sense of being fundamentally misaligned, as if I had been trying to fit a square peg into a round hole all along. I turned away from the view, sinking onto the edge of my bed, pulling my knees up to my chest. The silence was oppressive, filled only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs.
